findings.summary: 6-Gingerol and related gingerols inhibit thromboxane synthase and platelet TXA2 synthesis, producing antiplatelet effects additive with clopidogrel's ADP-receptor blockade; multiple case reports describe prolonged bleeding time and epistaxis in patients combining clopidogrel with ginger supplements, and perioperative bleeding risk is flagged in anaesthetic guidelines.
findings.citation: Bordia et al., Thromb Res 1997; case reports summarized; ESAIC guidelines 2022

findings.summary: 6-Shogaol, a dehydration product of 6-gingerol present in dried ginger, inhibits CYP2C9 in vitro; clopidogrel requires CYP2C19 and CYP2C9 for bioactivation to its active thiol metabolite; inhibition of bioactivation could paradoxically reduce antiplatelet efficacy while the pharmacodynamic antiplatelet effect of gingerols persists, creating an unpredictable net interaction.
findings.citation: Usia et al., J Nat Prod 2006

findings.summary: Ginger at culinary intake doses (0.5–2 g/day) has a long safety record and the interaction risk is primarily relevant at higher supplemental doses (>4 g/day dried ginger equivalent); patients on dual antiplatelet therapy should be counselled to limit ginger supplement use to culinary levels.
findings.citation: Borrelli et al., Obstet Gynecol 2005 (ginger safety review)
